title: Load-bearing rope habit
text: A near-useless knot habit that only compounds when the line is actually load-bearing under stress—practice knots barely count.

title: Quiet ledger sense
text: Instinctively tracks small unpaid favors and broken handshakes nearby; starts as annoying bookkeeping, not mind-reading.

title: Filing mishap compound
text: A desk-job filing habit that only grows when a real filing error would cost someone rations, work, or freedom.
examples: misfiled permit that freezes a shipment

title: Half-step afterimage
text: A short burst of impossible footwork useful for escapes; repeated use leaves deep fatigue, never full teleport.

title: Ashen oath sniff
text: Senses when someone nearby is hiding a binding promise or unpaid debt—only after witnessing a broken oath with cost.

title: Thread of weight
text: Briefly notices emotional weight stuck to an object or place; too many reads cause sensory overload.
examples: knife that remembers a kitchen argument

title: Rust touch
text: Slightly accelerates wear on a single tool or lock with prolonged contact—barely useful until carefully trained.

title: Ink memory flash
text: Perfectly recalls one short written passage seen in the last day—nothing more; forced double use brings headache.

title: False stillness
text: Hold perfectly still for a short count to dodge a casual glance—not true invisibility, just ugly muscle control.

title: Scavenger timing
text: Weak scavenger timing: finds slightly better angles on debris and dead systems only when risking unstable ground.
examples: pull a battery before the floor drops

title: Face-read bow
text: A cultivated half-bow that reads who must be greeted first; starts as clumsy politeness, compounds with real social stakes.
examples: wrong-order greeting that starts a debt

title: River craft hands
text: Hands that remember wet rope, nets, and river wood; growth comes from helping the village, not combat kills.

title: Dead-reckoning itch
text: An itch when a path is wrong by a few degrees; early on it is often false, later it tracks true after costly mistakes.

title: Tincture nose
text: Smells whether a tincture will spoil or bite; weak at first, dangerous if overtrusted.

title: Soft-lie tell
text: Notices one soft tell in casual speech—never full truth-reading; wrong guesses cost trust.
examples: eye flick when naming a price

title: Status-window static
text: Hears faint system static near interfaces; starts as headache noise, can later tease half-true menu edges.
